hero.title: Acute Pancreatitis
hero.subtitle: Understanding, recovering from, and preventing acute pancreatitis
hero.description: Everything you need to know about acute pancreatitis — why it happens, how your body heals, and what you can do to prevent it from coming back. Clear, trustworthy information to support your recovery.
hero.reassurance: Most episodes of acute pancreatitis are mild and resolve within a few days. Understanding the cause helps prevent future episodes.

sections.title: What Is Acute Pancreatitis?
sections.subtitle: Understanding what happens in your body
sections.summary: Acute pancreatitis is sudden inflammation of the pancreas. It can be painful, but most cases are mild and get better within a few days with hospital care.

content.text: Your pancreas is a small organ that sits behind your stomach. It has two important jobs: it makes enzymes that help you digest food, and it makes hormones like insulin that control your blood sugar. In acute pancreatitis, the pancreas becomes inflamed very quickly. This usually causes sudden, severe belly pain that leads people to the emergency room. The good news is that most episodes are mild and get better with a short hospital stay.

points.heading: Sudden onset
points.text: Acute pancreatitis comes on quickly, usually over a few hours. The pain is typically in the upper belly and may spread to the back.

points.heading: Most cases are mild
points.text: About 80% of acute pancreatitis cases are mild. This means the pancreas is inflamed but recovers on its own without lasting damage.

points.heading: Hospital care is needed
points.text: Most people need a hospital stay for pain control, IV fluids, and monitoring. Mild cases usually improve within 3 to 5 days.

points.heading: Finding the cause is key
points.text: Your doctors will run blood tests and imaging (usually an ultrasound) to figure out what caused the episode. Knowing the cause is the most important step in preventing it from happening again.

content.heading: What does the pain feel like?
content.text: The pain of acute pancreatitis is usually in the upper middle part of your belly, just below the breastbone. It often spreads straight through to the back. Many people describe it as a constant, deep, boring pain that gets worse after eating. Leaning forward sometimes helps a little. The pain is usually severe enough to bring you to the emergency room. Nausea and vomiting are common alongside the pain.

sections.title: Causes
sections.subtitle: Why acute pancreatitis happens and what your doctor will look for
sections.summary: The two most common causes are gallstones and alcohol. Finding the cause is the single most important step in preventing another episode.

content.text: There are several things that can trigger acute pancreatitis. Your medical team will do blood tests and an ultrasound to find the cause. In most cases, the cause can be identified. Knowing the cause matters because each cause has its own specific way to prevent it from happening again.

items.name: Gallstones
items.description: Small stones from the gallbladder can block the duct that drains the pancreas. Removing the gallbladder (cholecystectomy) prevents this from happening again.

items.name: Alcohol
items.description: Heavy alcohol use can inflame the pancreas. This usually requires years of heavy drinking, but it can happen with binge drinking too. Stopping alcohol is the key to prevention.

items.name: High triglycerides
items.description: Very high blood fat levels (usually above 1,000 mg/dL) can trigger pancreatitis. Medication and diet changes can bring these levels down.

items.name: Medications
items.description: Some medications can rarely cause pancreatitis. Your doctor will review your medication list. Common culprits include certain blood pressure pills, seizure medications, and immune-suppressing drugs.

items.name: Post-ERCP
items.description: ERCP is a procedure used to treat bile duct problems. Pancreatitis is a known possible side effect, occurring in about 5 to 10% of cases.

items.name: Idiopathic (no cause found)
items.description: In about 15 to 20% of cases, no clear cause is found after initial testing. Your doctor may recommend further investigation, such as an endoscopic ultrasound, to look for small stones or other hidden causes.

content.title: Gallstone pancreatitis and surgery
content.text: If gallstones caused your pancreatitis, your doctor will likely recommend removing your gallbladder (cholecystectomy). This is a common, safe surgery that prevents future episodes. It is ideally done during the same hospital stay or within 2 weeks. You do not need your gallbladder to live a normal life.

sections.title: Recovery
sections.subtitle: What to expect in hospital and after you go home
sections.summary: Recovery happens in stages. Most people start feeling better within a few days and return to normal activities within 1 to 2 weeks.

content.text: Recovering from acute pancreatitis takes time, but most people make a full recovery. Understanding what to expect at each stage can help you feel more in control and less anxious.

items: IV fluids to keep you hydrated and support your pancreas
Pain medication — your team will work to keep you comfortable
Nothing to eat at first, then clear liquids, then solid food as tolerated
Blood tests to monitor inflammation and organ function
Ultrasound to check for gallstones if not already done
Early eating is encouraged as soon as you can tolerate it — this actually helps you recover faster
Eat small, low-fat meals — your pancreas is still healing
Stay well hydrated with water and clear fluids
Rest as needed but try gentle walking each day
Take prescribed pain medication if needed
Avoid alcohol completely
Attend your follow-up appointment (usually 1 to 2 weeks after discharge)
Gradually return to your normal diet as you feel better
Most people feel back to normal within 4 to 6 weeks
Follow through on the plan to treat the underlying cause (for example, gallbladder surgery or stopping alcohol)
Report any return of pain, fever, or poor appetite to your doctor
Attend any planned imaging or follow-up tests

content.heading: Post-Discharge Recovery Checklist
items: Follow-up appointment booked with my doctor (within 1 to 2 weeks)
Eating small, low-fat meals and tolerating food well
Taking any prescribed medications as directed
Avoiding alcohol completely
Gallbladder surgery scheduled (if gallstones were the cause)
Know the warning signs that mean I should go back to the ER

sections.title: Preventing Recurrence
sections.subtitle: Steps you can take to keep it from happening again
sections.summary: The best way to prevent another episode depends on what caused the first one. There are clear steps for each cause.

content.text: Once you have had one episode of acute pancreatitis, preventing another one is the top priority. The approach depends on the cause. Your doctor will work with you on a plan tailored to your situation.

points.heading: Gallstones
points.text: Gallbladder removal (cholecystectomy) is the most effective prevention. This is usually recommended during the same hospital stay or within 2 weeks. Without surgery, the risk of another episode within 6 weeks is up to 30%.

points.heading: Alcohol
points.text: Complete abstinence from alcohol is strongly recommended. Even moderate drinking can trigger another episode. Talk to your doctor about support programs if stopping is difficult.

points.heading: High triglycerides
points.text: Lowering triglyceride levels with medication (fibrates) and dietary changes reduces risk. Avoid sugary drinks, refined carbohydrates, and excess alcohol. Your doctor will recheck your levels.

points.heading: Medications
points.text: If a medication was the suspected cause, your doctor will switch you to an alternative. Do not stop any medication on your own without talking to your doctor first.

points.heading: Idiopathic (unknown cause)
points.text: Your doctor may recommend further testing, such as an endoscopic ultrasound or genetic testing. Following general healthy habits still helps reduce risk.

content.heading: Dietary Guidance During Recovery
allowed: Lean proteins (chicken breast, fish, turkey, egg whites)
Fruits and vegetables (cooked or soft varieties may be easier at first)
Whole grains (rice, oatmeal, whole wheat bread)
Water, herbal teas, and clear broths
Small, frequent meals throughout the day
avoid: Full-fat dairy (whole milk, cream, butter in large amounts)
Fatty red meat and processed meats
Sugary drinks and sweets (especially if triglycerides are high)

content.title: Alcohol and pancreatitis
content.text: If alcohol was the cause, complete and permanent abstinence is the single most important thing you can do. Even one drink can trigger another episode. If you find it hard to stop drinking, talk to your doctor — there are effective treatments and support programs that can help.

sections.title: When to Seek Help
sections.subtitle: Warning signs that mean you should call your doctor or go to the ER
sections.summary: Most people recover smoothly, but some symptoms after discharge need prompt medical attention.

items: Severe belly pain that returns or gets worse
Fever above 38.5°C (101.3°F)
Unable to keep food or fluids down for more than 24 hours
New or worsening nausea and vomiting
Your belly becomes swollen, firm, or very tender
Yellowing of your skin or eyes (jaundice)
Feeling dizzy, lightheaded, or very weak
Dark urine or very pale stools

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.subtitle: Answers to common questions about acute pancreatitis
sections.summary: Got questions about acute pancreatitis? Here are clear answers to the things patients ask most often.

questions.a: It depends on the cause. If the cause is found and treated — for example, removing the gallbladder for gallstones or stopping alcohol — the risk of it happening again is much lower. Without treating the cause, the chance of another episode can be as high as 30% within a few weeks.

questions.q: When can I eat normally again?
questions.a: Most people can start eating a normal diet within 1 to 2 weeks after a mild episode. During recovery, start with small, low-fat meals and gradually add more variety as you feel better. There is no need to stay on a liquid diet once you can tolerate solid food.

If gallstones caused your pancreatitis, yes — removing the gallbladder is recommended to prevent another episode. This is a very common and safe surgery, often done with tiny incisions (laparoscopic). Most people go home the same day or the next day. If another cause was found, surgery is usually not needed.

questions.q: Can I ever have a drink again?
questions.a: If alcohol caused your pancreatitis, doctors strongly recommend stopping alcohol completely and permanently. Even small amounts can trigger another episode or lead to chronic pancreatitis. If gallstones were the cause and you only drink in moderation, you can likely resume moderate drinking after you have fully recovered — but check with your doctor.

A single mild episode usually does not cause diabetes. However, severe or repeated episodes can damage enough of the pancreas to affect its ability to make insulin. Your doctor may check your blood sugar during follow-up to make sure everything is normal.

questions.q: When will I feel like myself again?
questions.a: After a mild episode, most people feel significantly better within 1 to 2 weeks and fully recover within 4 to 6 weeks. Severe episodes can take longer. Be patient with yourself and follow up with your doctor if you are not improving as expected.

questions.q: What pain medication can I take at home?
questions.a: Your doctor may prescribe pain medication for the first few days at home. Acetaminophen (Tylenol) is generally safe for mild pain. Avoid anti-inflammatory medications like ibuprofen (Advil) or naproxen (Aleve) unless your doctor says they are okay, as these can sometimes irritate the stomach. If your pain is getting worse rather than better, contact your doctor.

questions.q: Can acute pancreatitis turn into chronic pancreatitis?
questions.a: In most cases, a single episode of acute pancreatitis does not lead to chronic pancreatitis. However, repeated episodes — especially from ongoing alcohol use — can cause permanent damage to the pancreas over time. This is another important reason to treat the underlying cause and prevent recurrence.

sections.title: References
sections.subtitle: Guidelines and resources used in this guide
sections.summary: This guide is based on current Canadian and international gastroenterology guidelines.

American Gastroenterological Association — Clinical Practice Guidelines on Management of Pancreatitis (2024)
International Association of Pancreatology / American Pancreatic Association — Evidence-Based Guidelines for the Management of Acute Pancreatitis
Canadian Digestive Health Foundation — Pancreatitis Patient Information
